title: Hand and Finger
purpose: DBQs collect clinician-documented medical information VA may use when evaluating disability compensation claims.

diagnosis_current_condition: What condition are you claiming, and what diagnosis has a clinician given you?
When did symptoms begin, how have they progressed, and what treatment/medications do you use?
What records, tests, imaging, labs, or specialist visits support the diagnosis?
nexus_service_connection: What in-service event, injury, illness, exposure, or aggravation do you believe is connected?
When and where did it happen, what unit/job/exposure was involved, and what records or witnesses can support it?
If secondary, what service-connected condition caused or worsened this condition, and what medical evidence says so?
functional_impact_severity: How does the condition limit work, attendance, physical tasks, concentration, relationships, sleep, travel, or daily self-care?
What can you no longer do, do less often, or do only with breaks, medication, assistive devices, or help?
Give truthful examples using frequency, duration, severity, and flare-up details.

rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.frequency_duration: How often symptoms occur and how long they last, including daily baseline and episode/attack patterns.
rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.severity_scale: Best, typical, and worst severity using a clear scale when appropriate, such as pain 0-10.
rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.work_daily_life_impact: Concrete limits: sitting, standing, walking, lifting, sleep, missed work, concentration, hygiene, chores, driving, relationships.
rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.flare_ups: How often flare-ups occur, triggers, duration, what gets worse, what you cannot do, and what helps.

symptoms_signs_findings: on examination, it is important to understand whether or not that pain itself contributes to functional loss. Ideally, a claimant would be seen immediately after repetitive
use over time or during a flare-up; however, this is not always feasible.
functional loss associated with repeated use over time. The observed repetitive use section initially asks for objective findings after three or more repetitions of range
of motion testing. The second subset provides a more global picture of functional loss associated with repetitive use over time. The latter takes into account medical
probability of additional functional loss as a global view. This takes into account not only the objective findings noted on the examination, but also the subjective
Optimally, a description of any additional loss of function should be provided - such as what the degrees of range of motion would be opined to look like after
repetitive use over time. However, when this is not feasible, an "as clear as possible" description of that loss should be provided. This same information (minus the
three repetitions) is asked to be provided with regards to flare-ups.
Instructions to the examiner for gap measurement: The position of function of the hand is with the wrist dorsiflexed 20 to 30 degrees, the
metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints flexed to 30 degrees, and the thumb (digit I) abducted and rotated so that the
Limitation of motion of the thumb should be measured with the thumb abducted and rotated attempting to oppose the fingers. Measure

Note: For any joint condition, examiners should address pain on both passive and active motion, and on both weight-bearing and nonweight-bearing. Examiners
Veteran severe pain or the risk of further injury), an explanation must be given below. Please note any characteristics of pain observed on examination (such as facial
expression or wincing on pressure or manipulation).
If this is the unclaimed joint, is it: Damaged Undamaged
For the index, long, ring, and little fingers (digits II, III, IV, and V), zero degrees of flexion represents the fingers fully extended, making a straight line with the rest of
the hand. The position of function of the hand is with the wrist dorsiflexed 20 to 30 degrees, the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints flexed to 30
degrees, and the thumb (digit I) abducted and rotated so that the thumb pad faces the finger pads. Only joints in these positions are considered to be in favorable
position. For digits II through V, the metacarpophalangeal joint has a range of zero to 90 degrees of flexion, the proximal interphalangeal joint has a range of zero to
100 degrees of flexion, and the distal (terminal) interphalangeal joint has a range of zero to 70 or 80 degrees of flexion.

items: Note: Ankylosis is the immobilization of a joint due to disease, injury, or surgical procedure.
Complete this section if the Veteran has ankylosis of any thumb or finger

If ankylosed, is there rotation of a bone? Yes No

sections.title: SECTION VI - OTHER PERTINENT PHYSICAL FINDINGS, COMPLICATIONS, CONDITIONS, SIGNS, SYMPTOMS, AND SCARS
SECTION VII - ASSISTIVE DEVICES
items: Brace Frequency of use: Occasional Regular Constant
Other, describe: Frequency of use: Occasional Regular Constant
If the Veteran uses any assistive devices, specify the condition, indicate the side, and identify the assistive device used for each condition.

sections.title: SECTION VIII - REMAINING EFFECTIVE FUNCTION OF THE EXTREMITIES
items: Note: The intention of this section is to permit the examiner to quantify the level of remaining function; it is not intended to inquire whether the Veteran should undergo
an amputation with fitting of a prosthesis. For example, if the functions of grasping (hand) or propulsion (foot) are as limited as if the Veteran had an amputation and
prosthesis, the examiner should check "yes" and describe the diminished functioning. The question simply asks whether the functional loss is to the same degree as
if there were an amputation of the affected limb.
Due to the Veteran's hand, finger, or thumb condition(s), is there functional impairment of an extremity such that no effective functions remain other than that
For each checked extremity, identify the condition causing loss of function, describe loss of effective function and provide specific examples (brief summary):

sections.title: SECTION IX - DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
items: Note: Testing listed below is not indicated for every condition. The diagnosis of degenerative arthritis (osteoarthritis) or post-traumatic arthritis must be confirmed by
Have clinically relevant diagnostic imaging studies or other diagnostic procedures been performed or reviewed in conjunction with this examination?
Indicate side: Right Left Both
Is degenerative or post-traumatic arthritis documented in multiple joints of the same hand, including thumb and fingers? Yes No
Are there any other clinically relevant diagnostic test findings or results related to the claimed condition(s) and/or diagnosis(es), that were reviewed in conjunction
If any test results are other than normal, indicate relationship of abnormal findings to diagnosed conditions:

sections.title: SECTION X - FUNCTIONAL IMPACT
items: Note: Provide the impact of only the diagnosed condition(s), without consideration of the impact of other medical conditions or factors, such as age.
Regardless of the Veteran's current employment status, do the conditions listed in the diagnosis section impact his/her ability to perform any type of
occupational task (such as standing, walking, lifting, sitting, etc.)?

sections.title: SECTION XI - REMARKS
items: Remarks (if any - please identify the section to which the remark pertains when appropriate).

sections.title: SECTION XII - EXAMINER'S CERTIFICATION AND SIGNATURE
items: CERTIFICATION - To the best of my knowledge, the information contained herein is accurate, complete and current.

recommended_user_inputs: Claimed condition and formal diagnosis, if known
Onset date/timeframe and service event, exposure, injury, or secondary condition theory
Treatment history, medications, devices, surgeries, hospitalizations, therapy, labs, imaging, or tests
Baseline symptom frequency, duration, and severity
Flare-up frequency, triggers, duration, severity, and functional loss
Work limitations, missed work, accommodations, daily activity limits, relationship/social effects
cheat_sheet_use.truthfulness_guardrail: Use this only to organize accurate personal facts. Do not exaggerate symptoms, invent diagnoses, or state medical causation unless supported by a qualified medical opinion.

script_templates.truthfulness_guardrail: Fill in only what is true. Skip wording that does not apply. Do not exaggerate, invent symptoms, stressors, exposures, test results, or medical nexus opinions.

default_options.frequency: Daily with a constant baseline and worse periods
default_options.duration: constant baseline pain with flares lasting hours to days
default_options.triggers: gripping, typing, writing, tools, lifting, cold, and repetitive use
default_options.limitation: grip strength, writing, typing, tools, buttons, opening jars, cooking, driving, and work tasks
default_options.treatment: NSAIDs or pain medication, physical therapy, brace/support, ice/heat, stretching, and rest
default_options.flareCare: ice or heat, rest, stretching, medication, bracing, and reducing activity
default_options.serviceEvent: [specific service event, injury, exposure, aggravation, or secondary condition]
default_options.workImpact: I have to change positions, take breaks, avoid lifting or repetitive tasks, and I work slower during pain flares.
default_options.dailyImpact: Chores, stairs, shopping, driving, sleep, and getting dressed take longer or require help during bad days.
default_options.mainSymptoms: hand or finger pain, stiffness, swelling, weakness, reduced grip, numbness/tingling if true, and reduced dexterity
default_options.evidence: service records, treatment records, VA/private records, medications, imaging/labs/testing, profiles, buddy statements, or lay statements if true

sections.title: Medical History / Onset & Nexus
sections.intent: Answer when/where symptoms started, what service event/exposure/aggravation may connect them, and what treatment has been tried.
sections.dbq_wording_to_hit: current diagnosis, onset, course, affected joint/body part, treatment history, and whether symptoms are still present; in-service event, injury, illness, hazardous exposure, aggravation, or secondary service connection timeline; medications, physical therapy, braces/supports, imaging, injections/surgery if true, and activity restrictions
sections.what_to_say_template: My claimed condition is {{currentDiagnosis}}. It first started or became noticeable {{onset}} after {{serviceEvent}}. Since then, the condition has continued with {{mainSymptoms}}. I have tried {{treatment}}, but symptoms still happen {{frequency}}. Evidence that may support the timeline includes {{evidence}}.

default_options.frequency: Daily with a constant baseline and worse periods
default_options.duration: constant baseline pain with flares lasting hours to days
default_options.triggers: gripping, typing, writing, tools, lifting, cold, and repetitive use
default_options.limitation: grip strength, writing, typing, tools, buttons, opening jars, cooking, driving, and work tasks
default_options.treatment: NSAIDs or pain medication, physical therapy, brace/support, ice/heat, stretching, and rest
default_options.flareCare: ice or heat, rest, stretching, medication, bracing, and reducing activity
default_options.serviceEvent: [specific service event, injury, exposure, aggravation, or secondary condition]
default_options.workImpact: I have to change positions, take breaks, avoid lifting or repetitive tasks, and I work slower during pain flares.
default_options.dailyImpact: Chores, stairs, shopping, driving, sleep, and getting dressed take longer or require help during bad days.
default_options.mainSymptoms: hand or finger pain, stiffness, swelling, weakness, reduced grip, numbness/tingling if true, and reduced dexterity
default_options.evidence: service records, treatment records, VA/private records, medications, imaging/labs/testing, profiles, buddy statements, or lay statements if true

sections.title: Symptom Evaluation
sections.intent: Answer DBQ-style symptom questions with exact frequency, duration, severity, triggers, and condition-specific symptoms.
sections.dbq_wording_to_hit: painful motion, weakness, fatigability, lack of endurance, instability, repeated use over time, flare-ups, and additional functional loss
sections.what_to_say_template: My symptoms are {{mainSymptoms}}. They happen {{frequency}}, usually last {{duration}}, and are triggered or worsened by {{triggers}}. On better days they are about {{bestPain}}; on worst days they reach {{worstPain}}. For example, my pain is present daily, gets worse after about {{limitBeforeStopping}} of use, and is aggravated by {{triggers}}.

default_options.frequency: Daily with a constant baseline and worse periods
default_options.duration: constant baseline pain with flares lasting hours to days
default_options.triggers: gripping, typing, writing, tools, lifting, cold, and repetitive use
default_options.limitation: grip strength, writing, typing, tools, buttons, opening jars, cooking, driving, and work tasks
default_options.treatment: NSAIDs or pain medication, physical therapy, brace/support, ice/heat, stretching, and rest
default_options.flareCare: ice or heat, rest, stretching, medication, bracing, and reducing activity
default_options.serviceEvent: [specific service event, injury, exposure, aggravation, or secondary condition]

subsections.title: Frequency & Duration
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Document frequency, duration, pattern, triggers, and whether symptoms are constant, intermittent, recurrent, or episodic.
subsections.what_to_say_template: My {{currentDiagnosis}} symptoms are {{frequency}}. They usually last {{duration}}. The symptoms I need documented are {{mainSymptoms}}. They get worse with {{triggers}}.
subsections.example_template: For example, my pain is present daily, gets worse after about {{limitBeforeStopping}} of use, and is aggravated by {{triggers}}.

subsections.title: Severity Scale
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Describe severity using measurable words, worst-day impairment, and any DBQ-specific severe-episode language that truthfully applies.
subsections.what_to_say_template: On better days, severity is about {{bestPain}}. On worst days, it reaches {{worstPain}}. Overall, I would describe it as {{severity}} because it interferes with {{limitation}}.
subsections.example_template: On a bad day, symptoms reach {{worstPain}} and I have to stop or reduce {{limitation}} instead of pushing through.

bullet_templates: Pain scale: best day {{bestPain}}, worst day {{worstPain}}
Motion/use: pain with bending, lifting, stairs, squatting, gripping, reaching, or walking if true
Repeated use: worse after {{limitBeforeStopping}} of activity
Signs to mention: swelling, locking, instability, weakness, stiffness, spasms, numbness, or altered gait if true

sections.title: Functional Impact / Occupational Assessment
sections.intent: Answer how the disability limits work, daily activities, relationships/social function, reliability, and worst-day functioning.
sections.dbq_wording_to_hit: painful motion, weakness, fatigability, lack of endurance, instability, repeated use over time, flare-ups, and additional functional loss

default_options.dailyImpact: Chores, stairs, shopping, driving, sleep, and getting dressed take longer or require help during bad days.
default_options.mainSymptoms: hand or finger pain, stiffness, swelling, weakness, reduced grip, numbness/tingling if true, and reduced dexterity
default_options.evidence: service records, treatment records, VA/private records, medications, imaging/labs/testing, profiles, buddy statements, or lay statements if true

subsections.title: Daily Activities
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Explain work limitations, daily activity limits, missed time, accommodations, safety concerns, and reduced reliability/productivity.
subsections.what_to_say_template: This affects work because {{workImpact}} It affects daily life because {{dailyImpact}} I can usually continue for about {{limitBeforeStopping}} before I need to stop, rest, modify the task, or get help.
subsections.example_template: A real-life example is: after about {{limitBeforeStopping}} of {{limitation}}, pain and stiffness build and I need to sit, stand, stretch, brace, ice/heat, or stop the task.

subsections.title: Flare-Ups / Worst Days
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Describe flare-up frequency, triggers, duration, worst-day functional loss, and what helps relieve symptoms.
subsections.what_to_say_template: Flare-ups or worst episodes happen {{flareUps}}. They are triggered by {{triggers}} and can last {{duration}}. During them, I cannot keep up with {{limitation}}, and I use {{flareCare}}.
subsections.example_template: During a flare-up, pain reaches {{worstPain}}, motion and strength drop, I avoid {{limitation}}, and I use {{flareCare}} until it settles.

bullet_templates: Work tasks: lifting, carrying, standing, walking, typing/tools, kneeling, stairs, or overhead work
Daily tasks: dressing, bathing, cooking, cleaning, shopping, driving, yard work, or sleep position
Limits: can stand/walk/sit/use joint about {{limitBeforeStopping}} before stopping
